name: My lawyer in NYC used ChatGPT for my case—can the other side get those documents?
description: Likely yes, unless lawyer directed it—NY 2026 ruling says AI docs aren't privileged without that.[1][3]

mainEntity.name: My lawyer in NYC used ChatGPT for my case—can the other side get those documents?

acceptedAnswer.text: Likely yes, unless lawyer directed it—NY 2026 ruling says AI docs aren't privileged without that.[1][3]
acceptedAnswer.description:
Yes, they likely can unless your lawyer specifically directed the AI use as part of representation.[1][3] The Southern District of New York in *US v. Heppner* (2026) held AI-generated docs unprotected if not at counsel's direction or reflecting strategy.[1][3] Courts apply traditional privilege rules strictly to AI, viewing it as a non-confidential third party.[3]

**Key factors:**
- Must be lawyer-directed for work product claim.[1]
- Public AI tools lack confidentiality.[3]
- Even shared with counsel later doesn't retro-protect.[3]

Ask your lawyer if they directed AI use and used secure systems; otherwise, assume discoverability. Disclose AI involvement per court orders in NY federal courts.[4] Get a second opinion on privilege strategy.
